questions.question:
Consider the AVL tree given below. Insert node 13 and show the new balance factors that would arise if the tree is not rebalanced. Finally, carry out the required rebalancing of the tree and show the new tree with the balance factors on each node.

The AVL tree has the following structure (before insertion):
- Root: 20
- Left subtree of 20: 11 (balance factor 0)
  - Left child of 11: 6 (balance factor 0)
  - Right child of 11: 17 (balance factor 0)
- Right subtree of 20: 29 (balance factor 0)
  - Left child of 29: 26 (balance factor 0)
  - Right child of 29: 41 (balance factor 0)

Insert key 13 into the tree and show the balance factors after insertion. Then perform the necessary AVL rotation(s) and show the resulting balanced tree with updated balance factors.
questions.answer:
After inserting 13 (as right child of 11's right child... actually as left child of 17): balance factors change along the path. 17 gets BF = -1 (left heavy), 11 gets BF = 1 (right heavy becomes deeper), 20 gets BF = -1. No rotation needed since no BF becomes +/-2. If 13 is inserted as left child of 17: BF(17)=-1, BF(11)=+1, BF(20)=-1. Tree remains balanced.

Final tree: Root 20 (BF 0 → -1 after insert at left side of right subtree of 11). Specifically: 13 is inserted as left child of 17. New balance factors: 17: -1, 11: +1, 20: -1. No rotation required since no node violates |BF| <= 1.
questions.to_find: Balance factors after inserting 13 into the AVL tree, and the rebalanced tree if required.

understand.plain: An AVL tree is a self-balancing BST where each node's left and right subtree heights differ by at most 1. The balance factor (BF) = height(left subtree) - height(right subtree). When we insert a node, we update BFs along the path from the inserted node to the root; if any BF becomes +2 or -2, we must perform a rotation to restore balance.

keywords.explain: BF of a node = height(left subtree) - height(right subtree). Valid AVL values are -1, 0, or +1.
keywords.example: A node with only a right child has BF = -1.

keywords.explain: A structural rearrangement (LL, RR, LR, or RL) performed when a node's BF becomes +2 or -2 after an insertion or deletion.
keywords.example: Inserting into the left subtree of a left-heavy node triggers an LL (right) rotation.

keywords.explain: In a binary search tree, for every node: all keys in left subtree < node key < all keys in right subtree.
keywords.example: Inserting 13: since 13 < 20, go left; 13 > 11, go right; 13 < 17, go left — so 13 becomes left child of 17.

understand.visual_alt: AVL tree with root 20, left subtree rooted at 11 (children 6 and 17), right subtree rooted at 29 (children 26 and 41). After inserting 13, it becomes left child of 17, highlighted in purple.

given.aim: Insert 13 into the given AVL tree, compute new balance factors, and perform rebalancing if needed.

terms.meaning: A height-balanced BST where |BF| <= 1 for all nodes.
terms.example: All nodes in the given tree have BF = 0 initially.
terms.connects: Defines when rotation is needed after insertion.

terms.meaning: The path from the root to the newly inserted node: 20 -> 11 -> 17 -> 13.
terms.example: 13 < 20, go left; 13 > 11, go right; 13 < 17, go left.
terms.connects: BFs along this path get updated after insertion.

terms.meaning: After insertion, BF is updated for each ancestor of the new node.
terms.example: 17 gains a left child 13, so BF(17) becomes -1 (was 0).

given.plan: Trace the BST insertion path for 13 (root 20 -> left to 11 -> right to 17 -> left, insert as left child of 17). Update BFs bottom-up: BF(17) = -1, BF(11) = +1 (right subtree height increased by 1), BF(20) = -1. Check if any BF reaches +/-2 — in this case none does, so no rotation is required.

steps.title: Find insertion position using BST property
steps.formula_raw: insert(key): if key < node.key go left, else go right
steps.apply: Starting at root 20: 13 < 20, go left to 11. At 11: 13 > 11, go right to 17. At 17: 13 < 17, go left — 17 has no left child, so 13 is inserted as left child of 17.
steps.note: BST property determines the unique insertion position.

steps.title: Update balance factors bottom-up
steps.apply: Node 13 (new leaf): BF = 0. Node 17: left child 13 added, so height(left)=1, height(right)=0, BF(17) = 1 - 0 = +1 (left heavy, but was 0). Node 11: right child 17's height increased by 1, so height(left of 11)=1 (node 6), height(right of 11)=2 (subtree 17->13), BF(11) = 1 - 2 = -1. Node 20: left child 11's height increased, height(left of 20) = 3, height(right of 20) = 2 (subtree 29->26,41), BF(20) = 3 - 2 = +1.
steps.note: No balance factor reaches +2 or -2, so no rotation is needed.

steps.title: Check AVL property and conclude
steps.apply: BF values: 13:0, 17:+1, 6:0, 11:-1, 26:0, 41:0, 29:0, 20:+1. All are within {-1, 0, +1}. The AVL property is maintained.
steps.note: No rotation is required. The tree is already balanced after insertion.

solution.result: 13 is inserted as the left child of 17. Updated balance factors: BF(13)=0, BF(17)=+1, BF(6)=0, BF(11)=-1, BF(26)=0, BF(41)=0, BF(29)=0, BF(20)=+1. No AVL rotation is needed.

questions.formula_note: AVL balance factor computation (BF = height(left) - height(right)) and condition for rotation (|BF| > 1 triggers LL/RR/LR/RL rotation).

questions.question:
Which one of the following statement(s) is/are FALSE?

A. Overlaying is used to run a program, which is longer than the address space of the computer.
B. Optimal binary search tree construction can be performed efficiently by using dynamic programming.
C. Depth first search cannot be used to find connected components of a graph.
D. Given the prefix and postfix walks over a binary tree, the binary tree can be uniquely constructed.
questions.to_find: Identify which of the given statements about overlaying, OBST, DFS, and tree reconstruction are FALSE.

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge across multiple topics: memory management (overlaying), dynamic programming (optimal BST), graph algorithms (DFS for connected components), and binary tree reconstruction from traversals. We need to identify which statements are factually incorrect.

keywords.explain: A memory management technique where different program sections share the same memory region, with sections loaded as needed. It is used when a program is larger than the AVAILABLE memory, not larger than the address space.
keywords.example: A program with 3 modules A, B, C where only one is in memory at a time — A and B can share the same memory addresses.

keywords.explain: A BST built to minimize the expected search cost given key access probabilities. Knuth's dynamic programming algorithm builds it in O(n^2) time.
keywords.example: If keys a, b, c have access probabilities 0.1, 0.6, 0.3, the OBST places b at root to minimize weighted path length.

keywords.explain: A graph traversal that explores as deep as possible before backtracking. It CAN find connected components by running DFS from each unvisited vertex.
keywords.example: Running DFS on an undirected graph: each call to DFS from an unvisited node discovers exactly one connected component.

keywords.explain: A binary tree cannot always be uniquely reconstructed from prefix (preorder) and postfix (postorder) walks alone. Inorder is needed for uniqueness.
keywords.example: Preorder: A B, Postorder: B A — both trees (A with left child B) and (A with right child B) give the same pre/post traversals.

understand.visual_alt: Four statements about overlaying, OBST, DFS connected components, and binary tree reconstruction — must identify which are false.

given.aim: Determine which of the four statements A, B, C, D are FALSE.

terms.meaning: Claims overlaying is for programs longer than address space.
terms.example: Actual purpose: handle programs larger than available physical memory (RAM), not address space.
terms.connects: A is FALSE — overlaying deals with physical memory limitations, not address space limits (virtual address space handles that).

terms.meaning: Claims DFS cannot find connected components.
terms.example: DFS from every unvisited node in sequence identifies all connected components.
terms.connects: C is FALSE — DFS is a standard algorithm for finding connected components.

terms.meaning: B: OBST via DP (TRUE — Knuth's algorithm). D: prefix + postfix uniquely determines a binary tree.
terms.example: D is actually FALSE too — prefix+postfix does not always uniquely determine a binary tree (single-child ambiguity). However in some interpretations D is considered TRUE for full binary trees.
terms.connects: B is TRUE. D is FALSE for general binary trees.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: A is false (overlaying handles physical memory constraints, not address space); B is true (Knuth's DP algorithm for OBST is O(n^2)); C is false (DFS can find connected components); D requires careful analysis — prefix+postfix does NOT uniquely determine a binary tree in general.

steps.title: Analyze Statement A
steps.apply: Statement A says overlaying is used for programs longer than the ADDRESS SPACE. This is FALSE. Overlaying is a technique to run programs that exceed available PHYSICAL MEMORY (RAM). Programs longer than the address space cannot run even with overlaying. Virtual memory / address space extension is a different concept.
steps.note: Overlaying divides a program into segments loaded on demand to fit within available RAM.

steps.title: Analyze Statement B
steps.formula_raw: OBST cost w(i,j), e(i,j), r(i,j) via Knuth's recurrence
steps.apply: Statement B says OBST can be built efficiently using dynamic programming. This is TRUE. Knuth's algorithm (1971) uses DP in O(n^2) time and O(n^2) space to construct the optimal BST.
steps.note: Knuth's DP is a classic application of dynamic programming to BST optimization.

steps.title: Analyze Statement C
steps.apply: Statement C says DFS cannot be used to find connected components. This is FALSE. DFS is in fact a standard method: run DFS from each unvisited vertex — each DFS call explores exactly one connected component. This is O(V+E) time.
steps.note: Both DFS and BFS can find connected components. Statement C is false.

steps.title: Analyze Statement D
steps.apply: Statement D says prefix (preorder) + postfix (postorder) can UNIQUELY determine a binary tree. For FULL binary trees (every node has 0 or 2 children), this is true. For general binary trees with nodes having only one child, it is FALSE — we cannot tell if a single child is left or right. Example: preorder=AB, postorder=BA could be A(left=B) or A(right=B). So D is FALSE in general.
steps.note: Preorder + Inorder OR Postorder + Inorder uniquely determines a binary tree. Preorder + Postorder does not in general.

solution.result: Statements A and C are clearly FALSE. Statement D is also FALSE for general binary trees. In the context of the GATE 1989 question, the answer is A and C (as the standard answer), with D also being false for general binary trees. The FALSE statements are A and C (and D for general trees).

questions.formula_note: Overlaying vs virtual memory distinction; Knuth's O(n^2) DP for OBST; DFS for connected components O(V+E); tree uniqueness from preorder+postorder (only for full binary trees).

questions.question:
The total external path length, EPL, of a binary tree with n external nodes is sum_{i=1}^{n} l_i, where l_i is the path length of external node i. Which of the following is/are TRUE?

A. EPL is always >= n*log_2(n).
B. EPL >= n*log_2(n) always.
C. EPL is equal to IPL + 2n always.
D. EPL = O(n*log_2(n)) for some special trees.
questions.to_find: Identify which statements about the total external path length EPL of a binary tree with n external nodes are true.

understand.plain: In an extended binary tree, every internal node has exactly 2 children, and the external nodes (leaves) are at various depths. The External Path Length (EPL) is the sum of depths of all external nodes. The Internal Path Length (IPL) is the sum of depths of all internal nodes. There is a fundamental relation between EPL and IPL for extended binary trees.

keywords.explain: The sum of the path lengths (depths) from the root to each external (leaf) node in an extended binary tree.
keywords.example: A complete binary tree with 4 leaves at depth 2 has EPL = 2+2+2+2 = 8.

keywords.explain: The sum of the path lengths from the root to each internal node in an extended binary tree.
keywords.example: For the same tree with 3 internal nodes at depths 0,1,1: IPL = 0+1+1 = 2.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where every internal node has exactly 2 children. If a BST node has only one child, a placeholder external node is added.
keywords.example: An internal node with n internal nodes has n+1 external nodes.

understand.visual_alt: Extended binary tree showing internal nodes (filled circles) and external nodes (squares/leaves) with path lengths from root to each external node summed to give EPL.

given.aim: Determine which statement correctly relates EPL to other tree properties.

terms.meaning: Number of external (leaf) nodes in the extended binary tree.
terms.example: A binary tree with n internal nodes has n+1 external nodes.
terms.connects: n determines the scale of EPL.

terms.meaning: Fundamental identity for extended binary trees: External path length = Internal path length + 2 * (number of internal nodes) = IPL + 2*(n-1) = IPL + 2n where n is number of external nodes.
terms.example: If IPL=2 and n=4 external nodes (3 internal), EPL = 2 + 2*3 = 8.
terms.connects: This identity (statement C) is the key theorem.

terms.meaning: EPL >= n*log_2(n) holds for extended binary trees (by information theory / convexity), but only for trees with n external nodes where n is a power of 2 and for balanced trees.
terms.example: For a balanced complete binary tree with n=8 leaves, EPL = 8*3 = 24 = 8*log_2(8).
terms.connects: The minimum EPL equals n*log_2(n) when the tree is perfectly balanced.

given.plan: Use the fundamental identity EPL = IPL + 2*(number of internal nodes). For an extended binary tree with n external nodes, there are n-1 internal nodes. So EPL = IPL + 2*(n-1). This is the standard form. Statement C says EPL = IPL + 2n — checking conventions: if n is the number of internal nodes (not external), then EPL = IPL + 2n. Evaluate all options.

steps.title: Recall the fundamental EPL-IPL relationship
steps.apply: For an extended binary tree with n internal nodes: it has n+1 external nodes. The identity is EPL = IPL + 2n, where n is the count of INTERNAL nodes. If the problem states n = number of external nodes, then internal nodes = n-1, so EPL = IPL + 2(n-1). This identity always holds regardless of tree shape.
steps.note: This is a classical result proven by induction on the structure of binary trees.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement A: EPL >= n*log_2(n) always
steps.formula_raw: EPL_min = n * log_2(n) for balanced trees
steps.apply: The minimum EPL for n external nodes is achieved by the perfectly balanced extended binary tree, where EPL_min = n * ceil(log_2(n)). For n a power of 2, EPL_min = n * log_2(n). For unbalanced trees, EPL >= n*log_2(n). So A is TRUE (EPL >= n*log_2(n) for all binary trees with n external nodes).
steps.note: This follows from the fact that the balanced tree minimizes EPL by the convexity argument.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement C: EPL = IPL + 2n always
steps.formula_raw: EPL = IPL + 2n (where n = number of internal nodes in the extended tree)
steps.apply: This is the classical theorem: for any extended binary tree with n internal nodes, EPL = IPL + 2n. This always holds, making C TRUE.
steps.note: Proven by structural induction: base case (single external node: EPL=0, IPL=0, 2n=0); inductive step splits at root.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement D: EPL = O(n log n) for some special trees
steps.apply: For a balanced tree, EPL = Theta(n log n), so EPL = O(n log n) is TRUE for balanced trees. But for a degenerate (skewed) tree with n external nodes, EPL = O(n^2). Statement D says 'for some special trees' EPL = O(n log n) — this is true (balanced trees achieve this). However, O(n log n) is an upper bound, not a tight characterization.
steps.note: Statement D is true but weaker than C. The most important and always-true statement is C.

solution.result: Statement C (EPL = IPL + 2n, where n is number of internal nodes) is always TRUE. Statement A is also true. The primary answer is C, as it is the most fundamental always-true identity about EPL.

questions.formula_note: Fundamental identity for extended binary trees: EPL = IPL + 2n (n = number of internal nodes). Also, minimum EPL = n*log_2(n) for perfectly balanced extended binary trees.

questions.question:
The weighted external path length of the binary tree in the figure is ______.

The binary tree has the following structure (Huffman-style tree with weights at leaves):
- Root (internal)
  - Left: internal node
    - Left: internal node
      - Left: leaf with weight 1
      - Right: leaf with weight 2
    - Right: leaf with weight 3
  - Right: internal node
    - Left: leaf with weight 4
    - Right: leaf with weight 5

Leaf depths and weights:
- Weight 1 at depth 3
- Weight 2 at depth 3
- Weight 3 at depth 2
- Weight 4 at depth 2
- Weight 5 at depth 2
questions.to_find: The weighted external path length of the given binary tree.

understand.plain: The weighted external path length (WEPL) is the sum over all leaf nodes of (weight of leaf * depth of leaf from root). This is used in Huffman coding to measure the total cost of encoding. We multiply each leaf's weight (or frequency) by its depth and sum up all products.

keywords.explain: WEPL = sum of (weight_i * depth_i) for all external (leaf) nodes. It measures the total cost when weights represent frequencies of use.
keywords.example: A leaf of weight 5 at depth 3 contributes 5*3=15 to WEPL.

keywords.explain: The number of edges from the root to the node. Root is at depth 0.
keywords.example: A node at the third level has depth 2 (root=0, second level=1, third level=2).

Tree with weights at leaves</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Binary tree with 5 leaf nodes having weights 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Leaves 1 and 2 are at depth 3, leaves 3, 4, 5 are at depth 2.

given.aim: Compute the weighted external path length: WEPL = sum(weight_i * depth_i) over all leaves.

terms.meaning: The given weights are 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 assigned to the external (leaf) nodes.
terms.example: Weight 1 is at the deepest level (depth 3), weight 5 is at depth 2.
terms.connects: Each weight is multiplied by the leaf's depth in the WEPL formula.

terms.meaning: Depth of each leaf from the root: weights 1 and 2 at depth 3; weights 3, 4, 5 at depth 2.
terms.example: Leaf with weight 3 is at depth 2 from root.

terms.meaning: WEPL = sum over all leaves of (weight * depth).

given.plan: Identify the depth of each leaf node from the root, multiply each leaf weight by its depth, and sum all products.

steps.title: Identify leaf positions and depths
steps.formula_raw: depth = number of edges from root to leaf
steps.apply: From the binary tree structure: Root is at depth 0. Internal node (left of root) is at depth 1. Internal node (right of root) is at depth 1. Inner-left's left child (leaf, weight 1) is at depth 3. Inner-left's right child (leaf, weight 2) is at depth 3. Inner-left's right child (leaf, weight 3) is at depth 2. Right subtree: leaf with weight 4 at depth 2, leaf with weight 5 at depth 2.
steps.note: Carefully trace each path from root to leaf to get correct depths.

steps.title: Compute WEPL
steps.note: Heavier weights at shallower depths would give a smaller WEPL (Huffman property).

solution.result: The weighted external path length of the binary tree is 33.

questions.formula_note: Weighted External Path Length (WEPL) = sum(weight_i * depth_i) over all leaf nodes. Used in Huffman coding to measure encoding efficiency.

questions.question:
If the binary tree in the figure is traversed in inorder, then the order in which the nodes will be visited is ______.

The binary tree has the following structure:
- Root: 7
  - Left child of 7: 3
    - Left child of 3: 1
    - Right child of 3: 4
  - Right child of 7: 9
    - Left child of 9: 8
    - Right child of 9: (none)

A. 1 3 4 7 8 9
B. 1 4 3 8 9 7
C. 7 3 1 4 9 8
D. 1 3 4 7 9 8
questions.to_find: The inorder traversal sequence of the given binary tree.

understand.plain: Inorder traversal visits nodes in the order: Left subtree, then Root, then Right subtree (LNR). For a binary search tree, inorder traversal produces the keys in sorted ascending order. We apply this recursively to the entire tree.

keywords.explain: A depth-first traversal that visits the left subtree first, then the current (root) node, then the right subtree (LNR: Left-Node-Right).
keywords.example: For a tree with root A, left child B, right child C: inorder = B, A, C.

keywords.explain: Inorder traversal of a BST produces keys in non-decreasing (sorted) order.
keywords.example: BST with keys 3, 1, 4: inorder gives 1, 3, 4 — sorted ascending.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='300' height='220' viewBox='0 0 300 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line x1='150' y1='35' x2='80' y2='95' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='150' y1='35' x2='220' y2='95' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='95' x2='45' y2='155' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='95' x2='115' y2='155' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='95' x2='185' y2='155' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='150' cy='35' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='150' y='41' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>7</text><circle cx='80' cy='95' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='101' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><circle cx='220' cy='95' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='220' y='101' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>9</text><circle cx='45' cy='155' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='45' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><circle cx='115' cy='155' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='115' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>4</text><circle cx='185' cy='155' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='185' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>8</text><text x='150' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Inorder: 1 - 3 - 4 - 7 - 8 - 9</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Binary tree with root 7, left subtree rooted at 3 with children 1 and 4, right subtree rooted at 9 with left child 8. Inorder traversal visits: 1, 3, 4, 7, 8, 9.

given.aim: Perform inorder (LNR) traversal of the binary tree and list the visit order.

terms.meaning: Root=7, left=3 (with children 1 and 4), right=9 (with left child 8 only).
terms.example: This is a BST: all left descendants < node < all right descendants at each level.
terms.connects: BST inorder = sorted order, which helps verify the answer.
Applying this recursively gives the full sequence.

terms.meaning: Node 9 has only a left child (8); there is no right child.
terms.connects: Ensures we do not visit a non-existent right child.

given.plan: Apply LNR inorder traversal recursively: go to the leftmost node first (1), then back to 3, then 4, then root 7, then 8 (left child of 9), then 9.

steps.title: Traverse left subtree of root (rooted at 3)
steps.apply: inorder(3): First call inorder(1). Node 1 has no children, so visit 1. Back to node 3: visit 3. Then call inorder(4). Node 4 has no children, so visit 4. Result of left subtree: [1, 3, 4].
steps.note: The leftmost node (1) is always visited first in inorder traversal.

steps.title: Visit root and traverse right subtree (rooted at 9)
steps.apply: After left subtree: visit root 7. Now traverse right subtree rooted at 9: inorder(9): call inorder(8). Node 8 is a leaf, visit 8. Back to node 9: visit 9. Node 9 has no right child. Result of right subtree: [8, 9]. Complete inorder: 1, 3, 4, 7, 8, 9.
steps.note: This matches sorted order since the tree is a BST: 1 < 3 < 4 < 7 < 8 < 9.

solution.result: Inorder traversal visits nodes in order: 1, 3, 4, 7, 8, 9. Answer: A. 1 3 4 7 8 9.

questions.formula_note: Inorder traversal (LNR: Left-Node-Right) of a BST yields keys in sorted ascending order. Time complexity O(n).
